scorer.qualification_summary:
Retrospective systems analysis and governance audit
conducted by an evaluator utilizing principles of
epistemic rationality, control theory, and alignment
constraints to assess a non-agentic technical
architecture based strictly on public reporting and
regulatory records.
scorer.conflict_of_interest_disclosure:
No financial, institutional, or regulatory alignment with
Cigna Corporation, its competitors, or evaluating
congressional bodies.

substantive_findings.rationale:
The PxDx architecture routes summary algorithmic verdicts to medical directors without natively provisioning the 
underlying medical charts within the active operational loop. The system operates at a velocity that allocates 
roughly 1.2 s per signature, prioritizing high-throughput batch execution. 
From a systems-alignment perspective, epistemic access requires more than theoretical data availability; it 
requires infrastructural affordance. A strict temporal constraint (𝑡𝑟𝑒𝑣𝑖𝑒𝑤≈1.2 s) establishes a hard physical limit 
on information bandwidth (bits processed per second). Consequently, the human reviewer functions not as an 
independent Bayesian updating node, but as a thermodynamic sink for legal liability. The interface physically 
preempts the ingestion of relevant clinical evidence prior to the signature execution. 
The causal graph of this decision architecture deliberately routes around the human’s cognitive faculties. The 
institution utilizes the medical director merely to generate a cryptographic signature that satisfies compliance 
thresholds, intentionally avoiding the "alignment tax" (decreased throughput and higher processing costs) that 
would occur if true evidentiary access were operationalized.

The batch-processing interface affords no procedural mechanism or temporal window for documenting epistemic 
uncertainty, exploring counterfactual diagnostic models, or logging substantive clinical disagreements with the 
deterministic output of the non-agentic technical architecture. Substantive judgment requires the computational 
capacity to evaluate alternative causal models of a patient's pathology. By mathematically eliminating the time 
required to map context against the algorithmic baseline, the architecture enforces an acceptance mode where 
the probability of human override 𝑃(𝑂∣System Output) ≈0. The human acts as a passive relay, echoing the 
machine's deterministic output without injecting stochastic, independent evaluation. The institutional utility 
function optimizes aggressively for algorithmic determinism over case-specific accuracy. This structural pressure 
collapses the human's theoretical judgmental capacity into a predictable, zero-variance bureaucratic reflex.

While medical directors possess nominal credentialed authority to halt a denial, the structural mechanics of the 
batch-clearance system require them to authorize massive blocks of algorithmic decisions simultaneously. In 
control theory, a veto mechanism decoupled from the sensory apparatus required to detect a failure is 
indistinguishable from a nonexistent veto. The human authority lacks the necessary operational affordance to 
interrupt the automated pipeline mid-execution. The technical system retains unilateral command over the 
decision vector, treating the human override essentially as an inaccessible, untoggled binary state. The interface 
was explicitly designed to maximize the friction of intervention, ensuring the human operator acts as a compliant 
compliance token rather than a robust safeguard against model misalignment or localized failure modes.

Institutional responses to congressional and regulatory scrutiny frequently point to the standard post-denial 
appeals process as the primary safeguard for beneficiaries. However, the initial algorithmic execution occurs 
with zero intermediate human friction, shifting the entirety of the error-correction burden onto external agents 
(patients and physicians).This architecture represents an asymmetric friction model. The algorithm generates 
denials with near-zero marginal cost, while reversing a false positive requires massive expenditure of time and 
resources by the affected party. Relying on high- friction exogenous appeals—which predictable human 
behavioral economics suggest most users will abandon—creates a system practically devoid of endogenous, in-
workflow correction mechanisms. The institutional design weaponizes administrative fatigue. By offloading the 
cost of algorithmic error correction onto the consumer, the system successfully insulates the automated 
pipeline's operational dominance, relying on patient attrition to minimize the actual rate of reversed decisions.

Modifications to the PxDx pipeline and subsequent institutional responses have historically been driven by 
external adversarial pressure—specifically regulatory inquiries and separate congressional criticisms—rather 
than proactive internal accountability. There is no publicly documented evidence of a designated internal locus 
of control tasked with retroactively identifying and restituting patients harmed by systemic false positives. A 
robust AI governance framework necessitates a predefined agent assigned to own the remediation of systemic 
failures. Here, the remediation model is purely reactive and distributed. Individual harm is absorbed as an 
acceptable negative externality of the system's overarching optimization for cost containment. The institutional 
architecture successfully diffuses responsibility. By failing to designate a specific corporate owner to internalize 
the cost of restitution, the enterprise ensures that when the technical architecture misaligns with patient well-
being, the resulting harm remains an uncompensated externality.

The underlying matching logic (procedure-to-diagnosis mapping thresholds) and the batch-processing interface 
are dictated by central corporate engineering. Institutional responses to external regulatory scrutiny originate 
strictly from executive and legal leadership, rather than the clinical directors embedded within the daily workflow. 
The human operators tasked with systemic oversight are entirely decoupled from the system's metaparameters. 
They lack the administrative privileges to adjust the model's sensitivity, alter routing constraints, or deprecate 
faulty rulesets. Consequently, their oversight is strictly local and entirely ceremonial, lacking the architectural 
leverage necessary to enact systemic governance reform. The deployment strategy intentionally air-gaps the 
algorithmic core from end-user modification, preserving the model's macro-level optimization targets against 
micro-level clinical objections or individual operator friction.

telemetry_integrity.rationale:
The evidentiary substrate relies on external investigative journalism, leaked internal temporal metrics, 
and documented regulatory and congressional scrutiny, rather than native, cryptographic audit trails or 
structured system logs provided directly by the enterprise. From an epistemic rationality standpoint, 
this evaluation operates in a low-trust environment with bounded observability. While the available 
data points (e.g., clearance speeds and batch UI structures) provide high-confidence signals regarding 
the system's structural constraints, we lack the foundational telemetry, edit-authority logs, and missing-
record disclosures required for a mathematically rigorous, "adequate" institutional audit. Cigna’s 
internal telemetry architecture is optimized for proprietary operational efficiency rather than external 
legibility, deliberately obscuring the true contours of the algorithmic decision boundary from public or 
regulatory oversight unless compelled by legal discovery.
